course_description:
Many modern applications of mathematics are carried out on personal computers or super-computers. This course will introduce students to the theory and advanced applications of scientific computing using Matlab. The first section of the course will focus on Taylor series, Newton method, methods of steepest descent, and solution of ordinary differential equations. In the second half, boundary value problems, finite-difference, interpolation and integration will be introduced.

Many of the most important practical problems in science can only be solved with the aid of computational techniques.

MATH3201 will give you an opportunity to develop a systematic approach to computer-based scientific/engineering studies. The specific learning goals are defined by the description of each weekly program, and by the assessment criteria.

MATH3201 will cover the following topics:

In the School of Mathematics and Physics we are committed to creating an inclusive and empowering learning environment for all students. We value and respect the diverse range of experiences our students bring to their education, and we believe that this diversity is crucial for fostering a rich culture of knowledge sharing and meaningful exploration. We hold both students and staff accountable for actively contributing to the establishment of a respectful and supportive learning environment.

Bullying, harassment, and discrimination in any form are strictly against our principles and against ﾠ UQ Policy, ﾠand will not be tolerated. We have developed a ﾠ suite of resources ﾠ to assist you in recognising, reporting, and addressing such behaviour. If you have any concerns about your experience in this course, we encourage you to tell a member of the course teaching team, or alternatively contact an SMP Classroom Inclusivity Champion (see Blackboard for contact details). Our Inclusivity Champions are here to listen, to understand your concerns, and to explore potential actions that can be taken to resolve them. Your well-being and a positive learning atmosphere are of utmost importance to us.

Additional timetable information
There are 3 one-hour lectures and 1 two-hour applied class per week for this course. These will be conducted in-person. In the event of campus closure, alternative arrangements will be advised on Blackboard.
Important: if you are ill, then do not attend any classes in person. Alternative arrangements can be organised – consult Blackboard for details.
course_aims:
MATH3201 will introduce students to numerical analysis and scientific computing by using numerical methods to solve scientific problems using environments such as MATLAB. The course contains a large practical component to provide hands-on learning experience.

The topics to be addressed in this course include:

learning_outcomes.description: Frame and solve a scientific problem using the appropriate numerical techniques.
Understand the derivation, error analysis, and expected performance of the basic methods in scientific computation.
Translate the numerical method into a computer algorithm and code
Model problems with differential equations and obtain and interpret accurate numerical solutions to these problems
Understand and implement methods for solving boundary value problems
Understand and implement numerical methods of solving partial differential equations
Understand and implement numerical methods of interpolation and integration
Demonstrate written communication skills appropriate for scientific reports.

assessment_details.title: Problem Set
assessment_details.task_description: Students will hand in solutions to a number of questions on the theory and applications of numerical methods. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Prepare your assignment as a single pdf file, either by typing it or by scanning your handwritten work. Name your file MATH3201_sxxxxxxx_surname.pdf. Ensure that your name and student number appear on the first page of your submission. Check that your pdf file is legible and that the file size is not excessive (aim for less than 2MB). Files that are poorly scanned and/or illegible may not be marked. Upload your submission using the assignment submission link in Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Solutions will be released on Blackboard 7 days after the submission deadline.
See ADDITIONAL ASSESSMENT INFORMATION for extension information relating to this assessment item.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Solutions for assignments will be released 7 days after the assessment is due and as such, late submission after 7 days from the original time submission is due will not be possible, regardless of approved extensions.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Lecture Series You will complete a 3-times weekly lecture series covering the theory and applications of computational methods introduced in this course.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 13 (02 Mar - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Applied Classes in Numerical Implementation You will complete a weekly applied class in addition to the lecture series.
